item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps I'm just not in the right mood for it, but this article did nothing for me. There are plenty of things that are done very well (TIL what mifepristone is), but I am not a fan of articles that heavily rely on other articles to make any sense, which this did - twice.

I'm glad that this works for a lot of people, but it doesn't for me. Might come back later and re-read some time.

item.title: Fuck, man. I haven't felt this bad in a while.
item.content:encoded: Just take my upvote and let me cry in my corner.

item.title: Oh
item.content:encoded: Well that hit like a brick

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, because I am apparently stupid, I completely missed the entire subtext (one might even say "text") of this until I looked at the comments. I mean, I got that this was imply Bad Stuff Was Happening, but I couldn't quite work out where the pin was supposed to drop.

(Possibly, as I continues my oddessey of creeping up the SCP list and only following crosslinks as the got mentioned - I got here from, eventually 2678, that's where I am right now…! - having just read 4220, maybe my mind was just leaning back away from sex a bit?)

Then, when thus explained to me (and Doing A Google of mifepristone, I got it.)

Ooer.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuuuuck, I'm disgusted and I'm uncomfortable, wonderful job, +1

This is so effective in causing feelings of disgust and discomfort. I feel like it works perfectly as the Department of Abnormalities' answer to 231 - while so much of 231 is censored, it's still a detailed, information filled article - we just can't see the information. 5832 here manages to conjure up an equally horrifying scenario but in that really vague, uneasy way that DoA stuff goes for versus the more explicit, clinical horror of the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone who feels similarly about 231, (it is very very flawed but I am still somewhat fascinated by it) I absolutely love this. I totally get why others wouldn't find this as effective as I do and still believe that the girl in this case is being written like an object, but a lot of this hit me a lot harder than 231. Thanks, I Hate It.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shit, dude. Like, I dont know what to say, but… damn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short, unsettling, and hits you like a brick at the end, even without getting the 231 reference. I get why many wouldn't like it, but I certainly do. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really want to upvote this article. I think that it's fantastic at what it set out to do: it tells a whole story through only a few objects. I love articles like that and this one pulls it off in one of the shortest, yet most compelling ways I've seen. I think it's genuinely one of the best written SCPs I've read on this site.

But I have to admit I'm pretty offput by this in the same ways I'm offput by 231. Even though this is better written, even though the little girl has more humanity and is, in more ways, a little girl, it still feels… empty. As others have mentioned, this still kind of treats her like an object. She's more humanized, for sure, but she's not really present. I feel like I didn't get a sense of her as a character. In a story with different subject matter, I would say that's less of an issue. But given what this is about, I can't help but feel it isn't that respectful. Novote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Incredible. Delivering a payoff with twists and slow reveals of information are extraordinarily difficult in SCPs because of how technical writing and suspense generally don't mix too well-too often, it feels like a story is being dragged along in a way that is unrealistic for an article.

This manages to tell a wonderfully gripping story full of subtext and little details that doesn't bury the lede, obfuscate information, or work through some contrivances. And yet, it remains extraordinarily good at revealing info little by little until the all too awful realization. Truly magnificent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You accomplished your goal with this article, and I feel sick.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm disappointed it took me this long to find this. Absolutely outstanding work. DOA is already one of my favorite canons, and this does a fascinating job of underscoring it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: delightfully discomforting mood in this one! things like the excessively leaded paint and the animal crossing cartridge do border a little on the indulgent but the overall arc of the piece is still strong. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man! That's a super interesting interpretation. The closest thing I can think of to that is SCP-1782, which is an article I have long been fascinated by. I'm glad that this article is provoking these kinds of discussions about possible meanings, and feel free to keep that as your headcanon!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is phenomenal. I'm not the first to have expressed this of course, I'm commenting for a different reason. When I was reading this article, before I read the discussion, I had a fairly different interpretation of what was going on here, and I know that if I had written something like this, I would be interested in hearing something like this from one of my readers. So this one's for you, AbsentmindedNihilist.

My original interpretation was that 5832 was some unknown woman's vague, half-formed idea of what her aborted child would have been like that somehow actualized into reality in the form of an apartment. This pregnancy was the product of a very abusive relationship (Hence the scribbled out prince Adam); the woman wanted to keep the fetus but was either forced to abort by the man or felt forced to abort by circumstance. The only thing she actually knew about the fetus was that it was female, leading to the fixation on superficially girly things like pink items and Disney princess merchandise. The powerful sense of misery and dread stems from the woman's pain and regret surrounding the pregnancy. Her offspring will never have a voice, so no-one in the apartment speaks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hate this article, in a good way. When I looked up mifepristone, it all clicked in the worse way. +1, fuck you

item.title: WHY
item.content:encoded:
bro I had to google mifepristone and…

WHAT THE FUCK, MAN

FUCK THIS ARTICLE

AAAAAAAAAAAAGGGGGGHHHHHH

she could not be heard she had no voice SHE COULD NOT BE HEARD SHE HAD NO VOICE NO ESCAPE TO IMAGINATION NO ESCAPE FROM HER TORMENT INNOCENCE STAINED NO ONE TO SAVE HER

WHERE DID THEY GO

;_; +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a very good article all the ways that people mention the dread that builds up with simple discription of items in a clinical tone. I think the most interesting part is the contrast it has to 231 in the way that I gives humanity to this poor poor girl going through something disgusting and horrible this is a very good article and the links to the DOA only made it better definitely a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tense issues I will fix, I'm always guilty of fucking those up. I might input some of the wording ones too, I'll sleep on it. Re: the other suggestions - I feel weird about making such drastic changes when it's already been up for a while and there's not a consensus that shit needs to be changed (see the faucet discussion earlier).

I think you were present when kaktus + I discussed DoA headcanons and interpretations - my version isn't incompatible with the familiar and the modern, but they don't interact with things like that without leaving a distinct mark.

The DS + Webkinz toy, and all of the other items were chosen because they are distinctly linked to a sense of time - namely the years when I would have been around this girl's age, as would many readers. This allows for a really tragic comparison between my/the similarly-aged reader's childhood and her childhood, because instead of there being absolutely nothing there, and thus having to imagine the lack of things like coloring books and video games and stuffed animals, you see these really pathetic versions of your toys instead, and that makes it so much more effective. It's meant to be linked to a given era because that makes the girl feel more real.

I'm happy with the ending and it will remain as it is. Thanks for taking the time to write this all out, it's recognized and appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who enjoys story-telling through the process of elision, I appreciate what this article is doing; that being said, I felt as if the atmosphere could have been built up much more fiercely. When you're working in such a small space, I feel like every phrase — every word — matters.

That being said, this article appears to be quite popular so take the following criticism with a prescription-grade boulder of salt!

Feedback:
– hide block

SCP-5832 is an apartment consisting of a hallway with two opposing rooms at its end. Those who enter the area are entirely unable to vocalize. Other deliberately-generated forms of noise like clapping or playing an instrument are possible, and other sounds are not affected. Evidence suggests that the anomaly was at one time inhabited, though the apartment seems not to have been entered for at least two years. A small metal placard under the apartment number reads “Department of Abnormalities.”

Right from the start, this killed the momentum of the piece for me. I feel like you could have accomplished a lot more with a lot less:

SCP-5832 is a two-room apartment located on the third floor of a tenement complex. Occupants are unable to produce vocal sounds. Although evidence suggests the apartment was inhabited two years before its discovery, an exhaustive investigation has yet to yield the identity of the prior tenant.

This is shorter, punchier, and produces an immediate tension: The question of who lived here previously (and why they occupied a space devoid of any voice). It also clarifies that only one person lived here.

(I'd also consider cutting the Department of Abnormalities motif; I think it runs counter to the theming you're going for here — the mention of modern contrivances such as a Nintendo DS and the mention of abortion later immediately centers us into the world of the familiar and modern, which runs counter to the theme of unfamiliarity and timelessness that I think the DoA typically evokes)

The leftmost room appears to be a child’s bedroom, painted pink. The paint has no anomalous properties, but contains dangerous amounts of lead and has peeled heavily.

The contents of the leftmost room are listed below:

My rule of thumb: Don't tell me it's not anomalous unless it's really important to tell me it's not anomalous. This sentence reads way more creepier when you take out the 'no anomalous properties' part. I also think you get more juice out of not even telling us this is a child's bedroom, but rather, letting us realize what it is:

The leftmost bedroom is painted pink; the paint contains dangerous levels of lead and has peeled heavily. Its contents are listed below:

Bam — we immediately get an image of a room with peeling paint. And by avoiding the phrase 'no anomalous properties', you plant an intriguing question in the reader's mind: Why is the Foundation telling us about the paint?

(If you say 'it's not anomalous', we instantly know it's not relevant; you're just setting atmosphere. But if you don't tell us it's not anomalous, we don't know what you're up to — which increases the tension and atmosphere.)

A Crayola 16-pack of crayons and a Disney Princess coloring book. It is completed normally, save that all images of Prince Adam from Beauty and the Beast have been colored over and scratched out using the red crayon. The red crayon has not been used anywhere else in the book.

You give me two items ('A Crayola 16-pack of crayons' and a 'coloring book'), and in the next sentence, you say 'It is completed normally'. Contextually, it's obvious what you're talking about, but there's a moment of lag where we have to figure out what you're referring to in that first sentence (the crayons? Or the book?). This is easy to fix, though:

A 16-pack of Crayola crayons and a Disney Princess activity book. All images of Prince Adam (from Beauty and the Beast) have been colored over and scratched out in red. The red crayon has not otherwise been used.

A few other notes here: "A 16-pack of Crayola crayons" reads more naturally, and I changed it to 'activity' book to avoid the repetition of 'color'. I also took out 'It is completed normally', because what does 'completed normally' even mean in regards to a children's coloring book? Just pointing out that all the images of Prince Adam have been scribbled out is far more interesting, imo.

(I would consider using black instead of red, btw — but otherwise, I agree with several other posters that this is an excellent bit — probably the highlight of the article, for me)

A Webkinz plush black labrador retriever. In several places, fur was crusted over with an unknown substance.

You switch tenses here (from present-tense in the previous paragraph to past-tense here). I'd also just flat-out cut 'In several places':

A Webkinz plush black labrador retriever. Fur was crusted over with an unidentified substance.

'unidentified' is a little more clinical than 'unknown' (and honestly, sounds a bit more like a forensic pathology report — which is what you're aiming for, here, I think).

A Nintendo DS and a cartridge for Animal Crossing: Wild World. The game was functional in all aspects save that when an NPC was engaged in conversation, no dialogue would appear in the resulting bubble. The player character’s personal commentary on achievements or actions was also missing. Other text was not affected. Based on the presence of certain time-locked features in the game, it had been played continuously for at least nine months.

Switched tenses again. I also like this bit (the 9 months bit is a nice foreshadowing), though it's one of the main reasons I feel like 'DoA' doesn't quite fit here.

A white wooden dresser. Contents of the dresser include a charm bracelet with one attached charm - a birthday cake with seven candles. Also found inside were several opened and partly emptied packages of Pull-ups disposable training pants, and three identical Disney princess t-shirts in a child’s medium size. All three shirts are heavily stained.

Switched tenses again. I also feel like 'a birthday cake with seven candles' is unclear (for a moment, I struggled to understand that you were describing the shape of the charm). I think this could also be a little shorter, except I'd tack on 'with an unidentified substance' (or 'with an unknown substance') at the very end (to harken back to the plush labrador retriever).

The rightmost room is a small bathroom with linoleum flooring. It contains a pink toothbrush and a tube of Crest children’s toothpaste, as well as a plastic “booster” stool under the toilet seat. No toilet paper or other hygiene products were present. There is a large crack in the bathtub’s sealant. The sink and bathtub faucet were functional and were not anomalous. The toilet functioned properly, though multiple water samples taken both before and after repeated flushing revealed trace amounts of proteins found in amniotic fluid.

The tense is all over the place, here — I get that you're talking about the point the anomaly was found versus its current state, but each time you change tenses, it's disruptive — you gotta cut back a little on the tense-flips here. Also, again, wouldn't say 'not anomalous' unless it's actually important (and I don't think it is).

Considering the importance of the toilet, here, I'd rephrase to make it punchier:

Water samples taken from the toilet reveal trace amounts of amniotic fluid. These traces persist despite multiple flushes.

And finally, the kicker at the end:

A plastic cup sits on the bathroom counter. The contents had congealed, but chemical analysis revealed it to be a mixture of apple juice and mifepristone.

I like this; I like how you make us google 'mifepristone' to catch on to what's going on here — it's a clever trick. That being said…

A plastic cup was found on the counter. The contents had congealed, but chemical analysis revealed it to be a mixture of apple-juice and mifepristone.

A few other thoughts (in no particular order):

  • I think this article would benefit greatly from a picture. A run-down apartment; peeling walls of pink paint; a shot of a bathroom counter with a plastic cup — something, anything to get me into the headspace of 'empty, decaying apartment where no one could speak'.
  • I feel like there's a way to be even slightly more clever with your ending. Find out a scientific way to say 'rotted apple juice'; switch 'apple juice' out for that, then include a footnote — and have the footnote say 'apple-juice'. This way, you have the stinger of 'mifepristone' (which prompts us to google)… but then you have the secondary stinger of 'What the fuck is that chemical? — oh it's apple juice' — which further cements the discordance you're aiming for, here (childhood/innocence vs the disruption of that innocence).
  • I'd consider switching 'paper cup' for something like 'spill-proof sip cup' or something similar — something that implies something a kid would drink from without explicitly saying it. That might be just on the edge of 'too much', though — YMMV.

Again, this article is doing well, so take everything I'm saying in stride. All in all, I liked where this article was going and what it was trying — but I don't feel like it quite sticked the landing for me. Either way, good luck with it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
honestly, i was just reading this as any other scip, and it was horrifying. the only problem is i apparently need to know what the Department of Abnormalities is in order to fully understand it, and clicking on the crosslink led me down a very unappealing rabbit hole.

but that's my sole complaint. this was otherwise amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know the funny thing is, I didn't think -231 when reading this, though it makes perfect sense. It reminded me a lot of Petscop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i don't think it's a reach, i truly agree with you on that. first, the story disturbs you… but then you disturb yourself realizing you filled in the blanks by yourself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: my heart hurts reading this, jeez. take my upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's no character, she's an non-entity. I don't know anything about her aside from having common child toys and some vague conveyed actions. There's no personality aside from "abused child". What was her thought process during her life? Why did she like these toys specifically? How did she act when she was abused? What did she think of her abuser? Did she try to escape the abuser somehow? Did she have any goals in life or was she resigned to her current life? Did she know abuse wasn't normal? There is no difference between this and 231 in terms of characters - both present the abuse of a child without presenting the child.

You could grab any child, fictional or otherwise, and put them into this piece and nothing would change because there is no character here.

Actual characterization is what the tale SCP-166 did to the original 166.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Environmental storytelling at its finest. One nitpick though. For better readability, I would suggest making the list of items bulleted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What scares me the most about this SCP is that so much stuff is implied but not directly said, forcing the reader to come up with whatever fucked up things happened to this child. It not only shows us how terrifying the situation already is, but how messed up our thoughts can be by imaging the specifics of said situation.

I may be reading into it too much, but nevertheless, I love this SCP. Easily one of my new favorites. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i couldn't disagree more. the article does all it can do to characterize 231-7, in my opinion, and gives her a lot of humanity; we see the other things she would have enjoyed, but we also see the ways that her abuse has negatively impacted her life. she's not reduced to an object but the impact of sexual abuse on a childhood isn't handwaved away either, and the way that it manifests, ruining the things she loves, is an apt metaphor for the encompassing effect that kind of trauma has.

i think the purpose of this piece is not to make us feel bad for a traumatized child from an outside or clinical perspective, but rather to give us a more inside perspective on what a traumatized child's life is like. it is certainly uncomfortable for it, but its attempt to do that does not inherently make the piece exploitative at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Niles continuing to be one of the best SCP writers on the site, bar none.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh Jesus, what the fuck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously, that sentence hit fucking hard. Up until that point I was thinking that she was the source of the anti-vocalization effect, but… really seems like the opposite is the case. Fuuuck does this article hurt.

item.title: Re: Little edit
item.content:encoded: Wikiwalk footers are generally added in a large batch after the current series is mostly complete.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the end, the child in this SCP is as much as an object as in 231. While it is miles more subtle and restrained than 231, this is still very much emotionally manipulating the reader into feeling bad for a faceless, non-existent character by taking advantage of human empathetic gut reactions. It feels uncomfortable to read because it is extremely easy to use children as narrative devises like this. A single sentence about something bad happening to a kid is all that's really needed to fire up a person's empathy.

I like the idea of DoA anomalies being bizarre, proto-versions of existing SCPs, and the parallels between this and 231 are interesting, but I can't stand stories about un-entities that try to tug at my heartstrings without actually characterizing a character.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ohhhh I'm glad this worked the way I wanted it to. Those were also two things that were around during my childhood - I had several Webkinz, and while I never played AC:WW, I do have strong affection for New Leaf, and that combined with the knowledge that many people my age did play it a lot as kids had me put it in there (over something like Pokemon SoulSilver, which was my first video game but a few years too late for the age I wanted to hint at).

The point of putting these specific sort of "time capsule" items was exactly so that readers around my age (of whom there are many) could have a point of comparison to their own childhood, and as such see just how much this little girl is deprived of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Caution! Rambling Ahead!

it doesn't matter what the stuff is on her doggie's fur. It could be semen, tears, vomit, anything. All that matters is that her only soft toy was dirty, and no longer soft.

Goddamn it. This line sums up everything about this piece. I hate this article, frankly. I hate how it makes me feel. It reminds me of when I was about 12, reading Room for the first time. I feel the same way here, this surreal feeling of melancholy and just an awful, shitty feeling. This poor girl.

The two things that spoke to me were Animal Crossing and the toy. I lobed both webkinz and Animal Crossing as a kid, (Still love AC now), and just imagining these things from my childhood put into this world makes me feel, shitty, I guess. I think it's because these were comfort things to me, and seeing them as comfort things to this girl, even in their wrecked state just makes me weep.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's something here that expresses something already passed, to some extent, but not necessarily concluded. It's a nice shape, pastel. I would argue that also, additionally, it is one note and fairly unvaried- the same sort of The End spoken repeatedly but in different positions, leading up to a singular Ending to bring them all together. The image is strong, and focused, however, so I do like it. It's the sort of thing that I greatly enjoy a fair bit too deeply for comfort for most people.

Here's an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know what to say. I refuse to think more about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's psychological. Yes, it's a practical alteration, but Procedure 110 Montauk, whatever it may be, is meant to generate despair. Having even the ability to express your suffering taken from you; that suffering becomes amplified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To clarify, I wasn’t suggesting that the article include dialogue from the AC:WW cartridge. That wouldn’t fit the article at all.

I was simply reasoning that the DoA probably wasn’t responsible for creating the apartment’s anomalous properties, since there’s no reason they’d erase the game’s dialogue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If the game allowed language, then the article would turn into epistolary fiction. I personally like it better this way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Thoughts
- Thoughts

The "mifepristone" bombshell didn't work for me, I'm afraid.

My immediate suspicion was indeed SCP-231, but I couldn't gel that headcanon with the amniotic fluid coming out of the faucets (which doesn't make in-universe sense,1 as a previous commenter has suggested). That, coupled with the scarcity of other clues, caused me to dismiss 231's involvement quite hastily - and I spent my second read confused about what was going on.

By contrast, I felt that SCP-3220, while arguably less ambitious and evocative, delivered its connection to SCP-173 much more deliberately - so there was a more immediate impact for me (and a hint of nostalgia to boot!)

Moreover, the anomaly itself (hallway where you can't vocalise) felt a bit contrived, and muddied the waters for me even more. My thought process on this went something like

  • Was it a fabrication by the DoA to quiet the screaming? (Probably not, since they wouldn’t blank out the dialogue from AC:WW)
  • Does it symbolise the repressive nature of the apartment? (Likely, but that's kind of hamfisted imo)

and in the end I wasn't really satisfied either with the anomaly or with my engagement as a reader.

What I do like about this article, though, is that it effectively conveys how the insular spaces of an apartment can be someone's whole world. That was certainly the case for me growing up, albeit not in so torturous a manner…

It's a very reluctant novote from me, but thanks for the read!

Footnotes
1. I guess it feels like a convenient namedrop that was done because it's vaguely related to the subject matter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh…oh dear. This is horror. Not 173, 47, 682. THIS is horror. The best thing about it is that, while its built up throughout the entire article, it's the final word, one word, that seals the deal. Everything, within a second, comes together and you're left shocked by that realization.

Honestly, what an amazing feat. Kudos to you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good work. So long as that particular legacy article continues to loom so large, it continues to demand interrogation — and I think you do that job as well as anyone could. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok yeah this is scary. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is absolutely brilliant
All the things said without saying them is stunning, well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is meant as a critique and as a challenge to what I find to be a deeply flawed article, though one that I still find fascinating.

That explains it. That makes so much goddamn sense now. I thought I was weird for not being as enthused about 231 as everyone else but now I get why it didn't click for me: it was missing that piece of humanity in favor of focusing on the Foundation's ethical strife. You've put into words why I thought the article failed and I didn't even know I disliked 231.

I keep liking this article more and more (in that "I hate this so much" kind of way).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OMG does this mean more DoA articles are allowed? breathes heavily

EDIT: Just followed those crosslinks. I am dumb.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yo fuck this article dog

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The faucet suggestion is a fair point, I'll consider that. Perhaps in the water that is found in the toilet.

With regards to your other thoughts - this is not meant to ride the coattails of the more famous skip which inspired it. It is meant as a critique and as a challenge to what I find to be a deeply flawed article, though one that I still find fascinating. It is intended to take that article's biggest oversight and dig deeper into that wound rather than letting it be covered up.

I'm glad this article created these feelings and intense discussion, I really wanted it to do that. Thank you for spending time on this, it means quite a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article has been the hardest vote I've ever made on this site, and not even because of how the content affected me.

EDIT: Removed my thoughts that no longer reflect my view on this article. My upvote still stands.

Thank you for your work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Considering this in terms of intentional connection to 231, this is an exceptional piece of commentary on how the Foundation's standard clinical tone is designed to remove all connection to the humanity of the people described in its articles. 231 describes a tormented child as an object; here, in an incredibly skilled and careful fashion, you've described a tormented child by the objects left behind.
In terms of the actual form and content of the piece, if it wasn't for the disturbing subject matter, this would easily become one of my go-to examples for insinuating a powerful story and instilling emotion in your audience. without sacrificing clinical tone. Every object described adds to the narrative in a palpable and unique way. +1 without a doubt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Contents of the dresser include a charm bracelet with one attached charm - a birthday cake with seven candles.

Well, here we go again.

In all seriousness, though, it's an impressive feat to make me terrified for a character who doesn't properly exist. +1, easily, and thanks for posting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was done very well in terms of bringing about emotion related to the piece. The overall mystery and unease caused by the theme and meaning of the article were more than enough for me to enjoy it.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This… this hurt.

+ show block
– hide block

It is completed normally, save that all images of Prince Adam from Beauty and the Beast have been colored over and scratched out using the red crayon.

No sentence has ever made me feel dread faster than this. Ever. There is a very distinct death of innocence in this line: there is no Prince Charming to save her - or if she had one, that he ruined her in a way that fairy tales don't matter anymore. There is no coping when your coping mechanism has been shattered before your eyes.

You can't escape with the dog - what was once soft is crusted to the point where you've lost something comforting. You can't escape with the game - there's no true contact to be made with the lack of speech.

There's just nothing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, fuck this (but in a good way). This made me so extraordinarily uncomfortable in such a short amount of time that I can't help but applaud your work here. The inability to vocalize kinda does it for me. All shades of fucked up when you start to think about it.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was my intent; I'm delighted it worked.

item.title: Little edit
item.content:encoded: Hey, if you don’t mind, I think it would be a good idea to add the next / previous scp code. I know the scp don’t exist yet, but just for later use…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Extraordinarily evocative. I actually gasped when I googled mifepristone.

item.title: A Threadbare Childhood
item.content:encoded:
chrome don't crash on me this time you fucker

This is my shortest piece to date. Perhaps not coincidentally, it took me under 8 hours to write - my shortest time spent on a piece ever. This evolved and grew into its full form so quickly it's honestly somewhat terrifying.

spoilers
– hide block

This is who you think it is. This was inspired by ARD's 3220. What 3220 is to 173, this is to 231. 231 is a blustery and dramatic piece about the world's worst act being perpetrated for the safety of all. The girl is a narrative object meant to stoke our fears about this horrible thing being done. She's not a person, because if the Foundation recognized her as a person, they couldn't possibly continue.

The DoA, in my mind, is a larval, base, shadow archetype of the Foundation. Ergo, this article is an inversion of the original. The DoA will not let you forget that this is a little girl. She is suffering the worst kind of abuse in a tiny world that consists of two rooms and a hallway. This does not erase her inherent status as a little girl.

She has a childhood - a washed out, neglectful, pitiful childhood, that has just enough attention paid to it that readers can compare it to their own younger years and find it lacking and terrible, but she is a child and she is real, and she lived here. The DoA will not let the Foundation forget that.

And to answer the question that I keep getting: it doesn't matter what the stuff is on her doggie's fur. It could be semen, tears, vomit, anything. All that matters is that her only soft toy was dirty, and no longer soft.

Huge thanks to A Random Day for helping me guide this piece into completion, and to Captain Kirby for the title and helping me punch up the ending. plaidypus and gee0765 also looked at this, and for that I am grateful. Thank you for reading.
